content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission

P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assitence • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statement of Economic Interest
(916) 322 5862                322 5660              322 5901              322 6441              322 6444

September 25, 1981

Eileen Perry
Security Pacific Corporation
333 So. Hope Street
Los Angeles, CA    90071

Dear Ms. Perry:

     This is in response to your request for advice regarding the
proper method of reporting employee contributions to Security Pacific
Corporation Active Citizenship Today Committee that are "earmarked" for
a specific candidate.

     When the committee receives such earmarked contributions, it
is acting as an intermediate or agent. I will first direct you to Gov-
ernment Code Section 84302, which sets for the requirements for persons
acting as an intermediary or agent in regard to the necessary
disclosure of the true contributor to the recipient of the
contribution. In addition, I have enclosed a previous advice letter
dated September 16, 1976, which explains the proper method of reporting
such earmarked contributions on the committee's campaign disclosure
statements.

     I have also reviewed the committee's 1981 semi-annual campaign
statement dated January 1 through June 30, 1981 with other members of
the staff and we find no problem with the committee's reporting of
earmarked contributions on that statement.

     If I can be of any further assistance, please do not hesitate
to contact me.

                                                      Yours Very Truly,

                                                      [signature]
                                                      Lynn Montgomery
                                                      Political Reform Consultant

Enc:  Letter dated September 16, 1976

CC:   David Pitman

State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

September 16, 1976

Mr. Reed Hundt
Latham & Watkins
555 South Flower Street
Los Angeles, CA 90071

Dear Reed:

This letter responds to the questions raised in
our telephone conversations of September 3 and 7, 1976. You
asked a number of questions concerning the reporting require-
ments and record keeping procedures applicable to a political
action committee ("PAC") which makes contributions to both
federal and state candidates.

When an employee designates a specific candidate
as the intended recipient of the funds, the PAC acts as an
agent or intermediary in the distribution of the funds.
Consequently, the PAC must comply with the provisions of
Government Code Section 84302 when it ultimately contributes
the designated funds to the candidate. This means the PAC
must disclose the "full name and street address, occupation,
and the name of the employer . . ." of the contributor, as
well as relevant information for the PAC.

When the PAC files campaign statements it is not
required to report receipt or disbursement of funds for
which it acts only as an agent or intermediary. However,
the treasurer may report receipt and disbursement of all
funds if doing so will facilitate his record keeping. If he
chooses the latter approach, the treasurer need not differen-
tiate the contributions that are intended for specific
recipients from other contributions received by the PAC if
the contribution is less than fifty dollars. However, if
any employee makes a contribution of fifty dollars or more
and designates the recipient, the treasurer should indicate
that the contribution was so designated when disclosing
the other information required by Section 84210(g).

If the record keeping requirements imposed by
Section 84302 are excessively burdensome, the PAC may want
to alter its procedures so that it merely acts as a conduit
for designated funds instead of as an agent or intermediary.
For example, the PAC is not an agent or intermediary if an

146

Mr. Reed Hundt
September 16, 1976
Page Two


employee makes a check payable to a candidate or candidate's
committee and the PAC merely delivers the check to the can-
didate. Even if the employee makes a check payable to the
PAC and the PAC endorses the check over to the ultimate
recipient, the PAC has not acted as an agent or interme-
diary. If these procedures are followed, the PAC is not an
agent or intermediary and is not required to follow the
procedures set forth in Section 84302. However, the PAC
should inform contributors that the candidate, not the PAC,
should be listed as the recipient of the contribution on any
campaign statement required to be filed by the contributor.

If employees make contributions stating that the
funds should be dispersed to "Democratic candidates" or
"Republican candidates," the PAC is not acting as an agent
or intermediary. Instead, the PAC exercises considerable
discretion to determine who the recipient of the contribu-
tions will be. Thus, Government Code Section 84302 is not
applicable.

This advice is not an opinion of the Commission.
If you disagree with the information contained herein, you
may request a formal opinion pursuant to 2 Cal. Adm. Code
Section 18320. If I can be of assistance, don't hesitate to
call.

Sincerely,

Natalie E. West
Staff Counsel
Legal Division

State of California

Memorandum

To : Jay Greenwood, Chief
     Technical Assistance and Analysis Division
     Fair Political Practices Commission

Date: August 25, 1981

From : Secretary of State - DAVID B. PITMAN, Manager
        Political Reform Division

Subject: Request for Interpretation of Campaign Disclosure Method

The notation on the attached copy of Form 450 filed by Security Pacific
Corporation Active Citizenship Today Committee leads us to believe that
this organization continues to await your advice.

Would you please determine the adequacy of their disclosure method and
advise both us and that organization.

Attachment

tf

Government Code Section 84200-84219

1981

under Section 84215 (b) or more frequent statements which receive cumulative contributions of loans of $100 or more from a single source must file Form 420

sections.question_synthetic: How should Security Pacific Corporation's Active Citizenship Today Committee properly report employee contributions that are earmarked or designated for specific candidates on its campaign disclosure statements?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC found no problems with the committee's 1981 semi-annual campaign statement reporting of earmarked contributions and directed the requester to Government Code Section 84302 and a prior 1976 advice letter explaining proper reporting procedures for intermediary contributions.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document is composite: primary response letter (81A054) with no explicit Q/C sections; includes attached prior advice letter dated Sept 16, 1976 providing detailed guidance on earmarked contributions; also includes Sept 25, 1981 memo from Secretary of State requesting FPPC review and the Form 450 statement that prompted the inquiry. Confidence reduced due to composite nature and lack of explicit question/conclusion sections in primary letter.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: How should Security Pacific Corporation's Active Citizenship Today Committee properly report employee contributions that are earmarked or designated for specific candidates on its campaign disclosure statements?

CONCLUSION: The FPPC found no problems with the committee's 1981 semi-annual campaign statement reporting of earmarked contributions and directed the requester to Government Code Section 84302 and a prior 1976 advice letter explaining proper reporting procedures for intermediary contributions.

embedding.summary: This document contains an FPPC advice letter from September 25, 1981 responding to Security Pacific Corporation regarding the proper method of reporting employee contributions designated for specific candidates, along with attached supporting correspondence and a related Form 450 campaign disclosure statement.
